revision.text:
[[arz:انجاكو جى]][[cs:Engaku-dži]][[de:Engaku-ji]][[en:Engaku-ji]][[es:Engaku-ji]][[fa:انگاکو-جی]][[fr:Engaku-ji (bouddhisme)]][[gan:圓覺寺]][[it:Engaku-ji (Kamakura)]][[ja:円覚寺]][[nl:Engaku-ji]][[pl:Engaku-ji]][[zh:圓覺寺 (鎌倉市)]] {{Short description|Zen Buddhist temple in Kanagawa prefecture, Japan}} {{hatnote|For another temple, see {{ill|Enkaku-ji (Okinawa)|en|Enkaku-ji (Okinawa)|qid=Q10927533|lt=Enkaku-ji (Okinawa)|1=Enkaku-ji (Okinawa)}}. Not to be confused with [[Enryaku-ji]].}}{{No footnotes|date=December 2022}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Engaku-ji
円覚寺 | native_name = | image = Stairs to Sanmon, Engaku-ji.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = The stairs leading to the ''[[Sanmon]]'' | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 409 {{ill|Yamanouchi, Kamakura|en|Yamanouchi, Kamakura|qid=Q8047902|lt=Yamanouchi|1=Yamanouchi, Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35.3377|139.5475|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Engaku-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[Śākyamuni|Houkan Shaka]] (Jewel-crown Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple, {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|qid=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain Temple (Kamakura)|1=Five Mountain System}} | website = http://www.engakuji.or.jp/ {{in lang|ja}} | founded_by = {{ill|Hōjō Tokimune|en|Hōjō Tokimune|qid=Q430964|lt=Hōjō Tokimune|1=Hōjō Tokimune}} and {{ill|Mugaku Sogen|en|Mugaku Sogen|qid=Q1187076|lt=Mugaku Sogen|1=Mugaku Sogen}} | year_completed = 1964 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Zuirokusan Engaku Kōshō Zenji'''|瑞鹿山円覚興聖禅寺}}, or '''Engaku-ji''' (円覚寺), is one of the most important {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen Buddhist|1=Zen}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|qid=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} complexes in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} and is ranked second among Kamakura's {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|qid=Q44372|lt=Five Mountains|1=Five Mountain System}}. It is situated in the {{ill|en:List of cities in Japan|en|en:List of cities in Japan|qid=Q735757|lt=city|1=List of cities in Japan}} of {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, in {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}} to the south of {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}. Founded in 1282 ({{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the temple maintains the classical Japanese Zen monastic design, and both the Shariden and the {{Nihongo|Great Bell|大鐘|Ogane}} are designated {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. Engaku-ji is one of the twenty-two historic sites included in [[:en:Kamakura's proposed World Heritage sites|Kamakura's proposal]] for inclusion in {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}}'s [[:en:World Heritage Sites|World Heritage Sites]]. It is located in [[:en:Kita-Kamakura|Kita-Kamakura]], very close to {{ill|Kita-Kamakura Station|en|Kita-Kamakura Station|qid=Q910286|lt=Kita-Kamakura Station|1=Kita-Kamakura Station}} on the {{ill|Yokosuka Line|en|Yokosuka Line|qid=Q1332317|lt=Yokosuka Line|1=Yokosuka Line}}, and indeed the railway tracks cut across the formal entrance to the temple compound, which is by a path beside a pond which is crossed by a small bridge. ==History== [[File:Shinpen Kamakurashi Kita Kamakura.jpg|right|thumb|upright=0.7|Drawing of Engaku-ji from the ''{{ill|Shinpen Kamakurashi|en|Shinpen Kamakurashi|qid=Q2397169|lt=Shinpen Kamakurashi|1=Shinpen Kamakurashi}}'']] The temple was founded in 1282 by a Chinese Zen {{ill|Monk|en|Monk|qid=Q733786|lt=Monk|1=Monk}} {{ill|Mugaku Sogen|en|Mugaku Sogen|qid=Q1187076|lt=Mugaku Sōgen|1=Mugaku Sogen}} (1226-1286) at the request of the then ruler of Japan, the {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|qid=Q477406|lt=Regent|1=Regent}} {{ill|Hōjō Tokimune|en|Hōjō Tokimune|qid=Q430964|lt=Hōjō Tokimune|1=Hōjō Tokimune}} after he had repelled a {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|en|Mongol invasions of Japan|qid=Q208156|lt=Mongolian invasion in the period 1274 to 1281|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}}. Tokimune had a long-standing commitment to Zen and the temple was intended to honour those of both sides who died in the war, as well as serving as a centre from which the influence of Zen could be spread. According to the records of the time, when building work started a copy of the {{ill|Sutra of Perfect Enlightenment|en|Sutra of Perfect Enlightenment|qid=Q3121475|lt=Sutra of Perfect Enlightenment|1=Sutra of Perfect Enlightenment}} (in Japanese, ''engaku-kyō'' 円覚経) was dug out of the hillside in a stone chest during the initial building works, giving its name to the temple. The fortunes of the temple have waxed and waned over the centuries. Its present form is owed to the Zen {{ill|Priest|en|Priest|qid=Q42603|lt=Priest|1=Priest}} {{ill|Seisetsu Shucho|en|Seisetsu Shucho|qid=Q11631550|lt=Seisetsu Shucho|1=Seisetsu Shucho}} (1745–1820), also known as Daiyu Kokushi, who reconstructed and consolidated it towards the end of the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} era. A particularly important year for these reforms and the history of the temple was 1785, the "500th Anniversary of the Foundation". In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} era, Engaku-ji became the chief centre for Zen instruction in the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region; [[:en:Imakita Kōsen|Imakita Kōsen]] and {{ill|Soyen Shaku|en|Soyen Shaku|qid=Q3481283|lt=Soyen Shaku|1=Soyen Shaku}} were successively {{ill|Abbot|en|Abbot|qid=Q103163|lt=Abbot|1=Abbot}}s in this period, and [[:en:Daisetz Teitaro Suzuki|Daisetz Teitaro Suzuki]] was a student under them. {{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|qid=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}} courses are still held in the temple, with open meditations sessions every morning (except for New Year's and early October) and every Saturday afternoon (except for August). There's also a sermon the 2nd and 4th Sunday of the month at 9:00 am, followed by a session of zazen. These activities are held at the main hall (Butsuden), Kojirin and Hojo buildings respectively. A four-day "Summer Lecture Series" is organized yearly in late July. == Architecture == Fire has damaged many of the buildings at different times, and the dates given below refer to the building of the structures currently seen. From the entrance, the buildings of the temple rise up a wooded hillside, with the major buildings in a straight line in the perpendicular zen style; the austere buildings and the trees blending in a satisfying overall composition. There are altogether 18 temples on the site. [[File:Engaku-ji (36530160165).jpg|thumb|left|The ''Sanmon'' (main gate)]] [[File:Engakuji-Gate-M9126.jpg|thumb|right|Emperor Fushimi's calligraphy on top of the ''Sanmon'']] [[File:円覚寺仏殿.JPG|thumb|left|The ''Butsuden'' (main hall)]] [[File:Engakuji-Buddha.jpg|thumb|right|Statue of ''Hokan Shaka Nyorai'' in the ''Butsuden'']] [[File:円覚寺居士林.JPG|thumb|left|''Kojirin'' meditation hall]] [[File:Engakuji-Bell-M9323a.jpg|thumb|right|Engaku-ji's ''Ogane'' (great bell) ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}})]] [[File:円覚寺弁天堂.JPG|thumb|left|''Bentendo'' hall]] [[File:EngakujiSenbutsujo20121008.jpg|thumb|left|''Senbutsudo'']] [[File:Engakuji-Shariden-M9239.jpg|thumb|left|The ''Shariden'' (reliquary hall) ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}})]] === Sanmon === The two-storied main gate, or {{nihongo|[[Sanmon]]|山門}}, was rebuilt in 1785, as part of the reconstruction works led by Seisetsu. A wooden plaque of calligraphic work by the {{ill|Emperor Fushimi|en|Emperor Fushimi|qid=Q373949|lt=Emperor Fushimi|1=Emperor Fushimi}} (1265-1317) reads "Engaku Kōshō Zenji". On the upper floor there are statues enshrined of {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}, the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|qid=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats|1=Sixteen Arhats}} and the [[Twelve Heavenly Generals]]. The roof is covered with copper. It is designated an Important Cultural Property of the {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. === Butsuden === The large modern {{nihongo|{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}|仏殿}} (; main hall;[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/b/butsuden.htm Butsuden　仏殿] at Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System) at the center of the Engaku-ji complex was rebuilt in 1964, after it was destroyed by the [[:en:Great Kanto Earthquake|Great Kanto Earthquake]]. The construction of this new building, surrounded by [[:en:junipers|junipers]], was made closely following a plan from 1573. It is dedicated to {{ill|Nyorai|en|Nyorai|qid=Q17052684|lt=Hokan Shaka Nyorai|1=Nyorai}} (''[[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] with a Jeweled Crown''), enshrined there, the main object of worship of the temple. This seated statue dates from the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. Statues of Bonten and Taishakuten in the same hall date from 1692. A painting of a dragon in the ceiling was painted by Tadashi Moriya under the supervision of {{ill|Seison Maeda|en|Seison Maeda|qid=Q3477943|lt=Seison Maeda|1=Seison Maeda}}. Above the front entrance there is a plaque of calligraphy from [[:en:Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kogon]] (1338–74) which reads ''Daikomyohoden''. === Sembutsudo and Kojirin === {{nihongo|Sembutsudo|選仏堂}} is a thatch-roofed hall for Zen meditation and a sutra repository, built in 1699. The {{nihongo|Kojirin|居士林}} is a Zen meditation hall for lay trainees (''Koji''). Meditation sessions are held here most Sundays, both for first-time participants and for the general public. Both building stand left of the Butsuden, and are open to the public. === Ogane ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}}) and Bentendo === Cast by Mononobe Kunimitsu in August 1301, the {{Nihongo|Great Bell|大鐘|Ogane}} of Engaku-ji is at 2.6 metres tall the largest of all the many temple bells of Kamakura (in fact the largest in [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]]. It was made by order of [[:en:Hojo Sadatoki|Hojo Sadatoki]] (1271-1311), after he confined himself in the [[Benzaiten (shrine)|Benzaiten]] shrine in {{ill|Enoshima|en|Enoshima|qid=Q989803|lt=Enoshima|1=Enoshima}}. This bell and the one at [[Kenchō-ji]] are the only ones designated {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} in that category of crafts in the Kanagawa prefecture (also the only ones from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}). A ''waniguchi'' gong from 1540 hangs in the belfry. It designated an Important Cultural Property. {{nihongo|Bentendo|弁天堂}} is dedicated to the [[Benzaiten (shrine)|Benzaiten]] shrine in {{ill|Enoshima|en|Enoshima|qid=Q989803|lt=Enoshima|1=Enoshima}}. According to the legend, the cast of the Ogane successful thanks to the protection of [[Benzaiten]]. Once every 60 years a grand ceremony is held between both temples. === Shariden ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}}) === The {{nihongo|Shariden|舎利殿}} (reliquary hall[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shariden.htm Shariden　舎利殿] at Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System), is a 3×3 hall, single-storied, ''irimoya'' style, with a pent roof enclosure, covered with [[:en:Hinoki cypress|Hinoki cypress]] bark shingles. It is the only building with the designation of {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} in the Kanagawa prefecture. The original structure, built in 1285 by {{ill|Hōjō Sadatoki|en|Hōjō Sadatoki|qid=Q3136681|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}} (1271-1311), was destroyed by a fire in 1563. The current building was transferred from the Taiheiji convent in {{ill|Nishi Mikado|en|Nishi Mikado|qid=Q15262396|lt=Nishi Mikado|1=Nishi Mikado}}, but it still dates from the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The structure is typical of kara-yo (''Chinese-style architecture''), also called Zenshu-yo (''Zen-sect-style''), introduced from China in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, with a style close to that of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}}. It houses what is claimed to be a tooth of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]], presented by the Noninji Temple in China to [[Minamoto no Sanetomo]]. === Other structures === Among the other buildings and monuments are: * Kaikibyo * Shozokuin * Kojirin * Hojo * the thatched Butsunichian (仏日庵) which is the burial site of Hōjō Tokimune; * Obaiin (黄梅院), a small thatched temple containing a statue of [[Kannon]]; *grave of film director {{ill|Yasujirō Ozu|en|Yasujirō Ozu|qid=Q55402|lt=Yasujirō Ozu|1=Yasujirō Ozu}}, marked {{nihongo|''mu''|無|''nothingness''}}{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hdi6mX9P9MYC&q=ozu+engakuji&pg=PA102 |title=Ozu's Tokyo Story (Cambridge Film Handbooks) |author=Desser, David |author-link=David Desser |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=1997 |page=102 |isbn=978-0-521-48435-0}} == Access and amenities == There is an admission fee ({{As of|2017|lc=on}}, 300 [[:en:Yen|Yen]]) for visitors to enter the temple complex, and additional similar charges to enter a few of the buildings. Booths selling tourist items are located near the entrance, below the Sanmon, and there are refreshment facilities in the garden of the Shariden and at the platform where the Great Bell is located, from where there are extensive views across the valley to other temple complexes in the [[:en:Kita-Kamakura|Kita-Kamakura]] neighbourhood, such as {{ill|Jōchi-ji|en|Jōchi-ji|qid=Q1504539|lt=Jōchi-ji|1=Jōchi-ji}} and {{ill|Tōkei-ji|en|Tōkei-ji|qid=Q536925|lt=Tōkei-ji|1=Tōkei-ji}} (another temple of the Engaku-ji school). == Annual Events == * Nehane (Shakyamuni Tathāgata's Nirvana Ceremony) February 15 * Gotane (Birthday of Shakyamuni Tathāgata) and Hana Matsuri (Flower Festival) April 8 * Kaisanki (Anniversary of the Founder's death) October 3 * Darumaki (Anniversary of Bodhidharma's death) October 15 * Homotsu Kazeire (Treasures Exhibition) around November 3 (three days) * Jodoe (Anniversary of Shakyamuni Tathāgata's Enlightenment) December 8 == See also == * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|qid=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} {{Commons category|Engaku-ji}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== *Richmond, S., & Dodd, J. (2005). ''Rough guide to Japan'', 3rd edn. New York: Rough Guides. *Information leaflet issued by the temple authorities ==External links== *{{Wikivoyage-inline|Kamakura}} *[https://www.engakuji.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/kitakamakura/kitakamakura_1.htm Engakuji Temple] at [http://www.kcn-net.org/e_kama_history/ Kamakura:　History & Historic Sites] *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110514103625/http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~QM9T-KNDU/engakuji.htm Engakuji] (overview) {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Engaku-ji]] {{wikidata link|Q954860}} [[Category:1280s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1282 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kamakura, Kanagawa]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Rinzai temples]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
